mission.title: Install and Configure Akri on Kubernetes
mission.description: Akri is a Kubernetes Resource Interface for the Edge that allows you to expose heterogeneous leaf devices as resources in a Kubernetes cluster. Installing Akri enables you to utilize devices like IP cameras and USB devices seamlessly within your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the Akri Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Akri Helm repository to your local Helm setup to access the Akri charts.
```bash
helm repo add akri https://project-akri.github.io/akri-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Akri using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install Akri in a dedicated namespace. This command will install Akri with the release name 'akri'.
```bash
helm install akri akri/akri --namespace akri --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Akri pods are running successfully by executing the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace akri
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance, configure resource limits for the Akri deployment. Edit the Akri deployment as follows:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment akri --namespace akri
```
In the editor, set the resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for Akri
steps.description:
Create a Role and RoleBinding for Akri to ensure it has the necessary permissions:
```bash
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  namespace: akri
  name: akri-role
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/status"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
EOF

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: akri-role-binding
  namespace: akri
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: akri-role
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: default
  namespace: akri
EOF
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Akri will show the Akri pods running in the 'akri' namespace. You can also verify that the resource limits and RBAC settings are correctly applied by checking the deployment and role bindings.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add akri https://project-akri.github.io/akri-charts
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Akri release from the Kubernetes cluster using Helm. This will remove the deployment and associated resources managed by Helm.
```bash
helm uninstall akri --namespace akri
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources that were created during the installation of Akri. This ensures that all resources are cleaned up properly.
```bash
kubectl delete crd akriresources.akri.dev
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the Akri namespace to remove all remaining resources associated with Akri. After deletion, verify that the namespace has been removed.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace akri
kubectl get namespaces | grep akri
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, create a backup of the current state of the Akri deployment. This includes saving the current configuration and any important data.
```bash
kubectl get deployment akri --namespace akri -o yaml > akri-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, then run the upgrade command to update the Akri installation to the latest version.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade akri akri/akri --namespace akri
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if the Akri pods are running successfully after the upgrade. This ensures that the upgrade was successful and that the application is functioning as expected.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace akri
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Akri pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs of the pods to diagnose the issue. This can indicate problems with the application or its configuration.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace akri
```

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permissions issues
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter permission denied errors, verify that the Role and RoleBinding for Akri are correctly configured. Check the current roles and bindings in the namespace.
```bash
kubectl get role -n akri
kubectl get rolebinding -n akri
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being killed due to Out Of Memory (OOM) errors, consider adjusting the resource limits set for the Akri deployment. Check the events for the pods to see if OOMKilled is reported.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace akri
```

troubleshooting.title: Akri not recognizing devices
troubleshooting.description:
If Akri is not recognizing devices, ensure that the device plugins are correctly configured and that the devices are available on the nodes. Check the Akri logs for any errors related to device discovery.
```bash
kubectl logs <akri-pod-name> --namespace akri
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, and that both Helm and kubectl are installed on your local machine.
